article_title: Oral Compound Mic-628 Resets the Body Clock and Cuts Jet Lag Recovery Nearly in Half in Mice

content_preview.title: Oral Compound Mic-628 Resets the Body Clock and Cuts Jet Lag Recovery Nearly in Half in Mice
content_preview.summary: A Japanese research team has identified a small molecule called Mic-628 that advances the mammalian circadian clock by activating the Period1 gene through a previously unexploited molecular pathway, cutting simulated jet lag recovery from seven days to four in mice after a single oral dose.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A multi-institutional Japanese research team has identified a small molecule that can reliably advance the mammalian body clock, offering a fundamentally different approach to resetting circadian rhythms compared with existing interventions such as light therapy or melatonin. The compound, designated Mic-628, shortened jet lag recovery from seven days to four days in mice after a single oral dose, [according to ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/02/260208011026...

recommendations: 1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-written News article at 679 words covering a novel circadian rhythm compound. Clear explanation of the molecular mechanism (CRY1 binding, Per1 activation at dual E-box) without oversimplification. Good inclusion of limitations section noting mouse-only data and differences with human physiology.
editor_notes.source_verification: Source 1 (sciencedaily.com): Confirmed all key claims — Mic-628 compound, 7-day to 4-day recovery reduction, single oral dose, CRY1 binding mechanism, CLOCK-BMAL1-CRY1-Mic-628 complex, Per1 activation at dual E-box, timing-independent property, 6-hour phase advance mouse model, researcher names (Tei H. of Kanazawa, Takahata Y. of Osaka, Numano R. of Toyohashi, Uriu K. of IST), PNAS publication. Source 2 (pnas.org): Bot-blocked (HTTP 403) — PNAS paper not directly accessible, but its existence and key findings confirmed by the ScienceDaily press release which cites PNAS 2026;123(4).
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified against ScienceDaily source: Mic-628 mechanism, recovery time reduction, timing-proof property, researcher identities and affiliations. The 43% reduction calculation (7 to 4 days) is mathematically correct. The 2017 Nobel Prize attribution to Hall, Rosbash, and Young for the Period gene is factually correct.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and measured. The article appropriately qualifies the findings as preclinical (mice only) and does not overstate the compound's potential for human use. The limitations section is well-balanced.
editor_notes.originality: Novel topic. No prior coverage of Mic-628 or circadian rhythm drug research found in the archive.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality science news article with verified primary source and appropriate caveats. Ready for publication.
